teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the visualization outcome students want to see, then explain the chain of files and publishers that makes it appear.
teaching_arc.progression: Move from the model description, to launch and visualization, to TF inspection and debugging.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to explain where the robot model in RViz comes from and how to inspect the transform tree behind it.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Help students understand how robot description, visualization, and transforms fit together so later debugging feels intuitive instead of mysterious.
topic_takeaways: Students should connect URDF/Xacro, robot_state_publisher, RViz, and TF2 into one pipeline.
Students should understand links, joints, and transform trees as visual reasoning tools.
Students should be able to verify a robot model and inspect transforms with the right tools.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Keep returning to the visualization pipeline so commands do not feel isolated.
Use scene/object hints only where they clarify frames, links, joints, or transform relationships.
transition_style: Each section should feel like the next layer of the visualization stack: describe the robot, launch it, inspect it, then debug the transforms behind it.

slides.narration_seed: Today is about visualization and frames. If you can’t visualize the robot and TF tree, debugging Nav2 later becomes painful.
must_say: TF2 keeps track of where one frame is relative to another frame over time.
If you can explain the visualization pipeline, you can debug a surprising amount of ROS 2 behavior later.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by promising that the lesson will explain where the robot in RViz comes from and how to reason about its frames.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to give students a clean route through the pipeline before any setup commands appear.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Frame the topic as the visualization and frame-debugging foundation students need before later Nav2 work.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel that URDF, RViz, and TF2 belong to one story, not three unrelated tools.
slides.scene_hint: Use a TurtleBot or small car with attached coordinate frames so students can see relative transforms instead of imagining them abstractly.

slides.narration_seed: Use Today to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Robot model files: URDF and Xacro
Clone + build TurtleBot3 from source (reuse later)
Run TB3 launch: state publisher + RViz2
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from URDF/Xacro + RViz + TF2 (TB3 Waffle Pi) into Today so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into The visualization pipeline (mental model) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Today to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Today connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Today sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for The visualization pipeline (mental model) using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: URDF/Xacro describes the robot structure (links + joints)
robot_state_publisher publishes the TF frames from the model
RViz2 uses TF + robot_description to draw the robot
If you see no robot in RViz: check Fixed Frame + TF
must_say: URDF or Xacro describes the robot, robot_state_publisher turns that structure into transforms, and RViz uses those transforms to visualize the robot.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Start from a clean terminal (and use aliases) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often mix up the robot model file, the node that publishes transforms, and the RViz display that visualizes the result.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for The visualization pipeline (mental model) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep The visualization pipeline (mental model) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain the URDF to TF to RViz pipeline in one sentence.
slides.scene_hint: Show the robot description feeding robot_state_publisher, then feeding TF and RViz, so the pipeline reads as one flow.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for URDF = Links + Joints (robot structure) using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Link = rigid body (base, wheel, lidar, camera…)
URDF forms a tree (parent → child links)
Each joint has an axis + an origin (where it attaches)
must_say: Links are rigid bodies, and joints define how those bodies connect or move.
Links are the rigid bodies. Joints are the connections that define how those bodies relate or move.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use URDF = Links + Joints (robot structure) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once URDF = Links + Joints (robot structure) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
Students often think links and joints are just XML tags, not physical modeling concepts.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for URDF = Links + Joints (robot structure) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly understand that links are rigid bodies and joints are the relationships between them.
slides.scene_hint: Bring in a TurtleBot silhouette with links and joints highlighted so students see rigid bodies and connections as separate concepts.

slide_ref.source_title: Common joint types (you will see these in TB3)

slides.narration_seed: the joint definition + joint_state values → robot_state_publisher computes TF for each link.
must_cover: fixed: no motion (sensor mounted to base)
must_say: Links are rigid bodies, and joints define how those bodies connect or move.

slides.narration_seed: Define Xacro (why TB3 uses it) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Change one parameter → regenerate robot description
TB3 uses Xacro to handle variants (burger/waffle)
must_say: Xacro helps us generate robot descriptions cleanly instead of copying large URDF blocks by hand.
slides.transition_from_previous: After students understand the robot structure, show why we do not want to hand-copy that structure for every robot variant.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Xacro makes sense, move into the launch steps that turn the description into something visible.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
Students may think Xacro is a completely different model format instead of a convenient way to generate URDF.
slides.explanation_style: Define Xacro (why TB3 uses it) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave understanding Xacro as a maintainability tool, not an extra hurdle.
slides.scene_hint: Bring in a TurtleBot silhouette with links and joints highlighted so the robot description reads as structure, not just XML.

slides.narration_seed: Teach Visualizing the Robot through the visual or interactive material, narrating what students should notice, why it matters, and how it connects to the topic goal.
must_cover: 1. In RViz, look at Global Options.
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for TF2 = Who is where? using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_say: TF2 keeps track of where one frame is relative to another frame over time.
TF2 is the system that keeps track of where frames are relative to each other over time.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use TF2 = Who is where? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once TF2 = Who is where? is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students often hear transform vocabulary without forming a clear picture of one frame relative to another.
Students often hear transform vocabulary without understanding that every transform answers a relative-where question.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for TF2 = Who is where? first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to say TF2 tells us where one frame is relative to another frame.
slides.scene_hint: Use a moving car or TurtleBot with attached coordinate frames to show that TF answers where one frame is relative to another.

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for How to read tf2_echo using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Translation: The distance in meters [X, Y, Z].
Rotation: The orientation in Quaternions or RPY (Roll, Pitch, Yaw).
Exercise: Look at the Y-value for the left wheel. Is it positive or negative?
Exercise: Move the sliders in the GUI and watch the Rotation values change live!
must_say: TF2 keeps track of where one frame is relative to another frame over time.
slides.transition_from_previous: Once students can request a transform, help them read the numbers without treating them as random output.
slides.transition_to_next: Finish by turning the lesson into a checklist students can reuse when they work alone.
likely_student_confusion: Students often hear transform vocabulary without forming a clear picture of one frame relative to another.
Students may see tf2_echo output as raw numbers without understanding which frame pair or direction those numbers describe.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for How to read tf2_echo first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to say what frame pair tf2_echo is reporting and what the numbers mean physically.
slides.scene_hint: Use a TurtleBot or small car with attached coordinate frames so students can see relative transforms instead of imagining them abstractly.

slides.narration_seed: Use Session 05 Checklist as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: 3. RViz: Set Fixed Frame to base_link.
Students should connect URDF/Xacro, robot_state_publisher, RViz, and TF2 into one pipeline.
must_say: By the end of this session, you should be able to describe the robot, launch the visualization, and inspect the transform tree with purpose.
slides.transition_from_previous: Collect the model, visualization, and transform ideas into one final self-check.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by telling students what they should be able to verify on their own before the next session.

slides.story_hint: Keep Session 05 Checklist connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave knowing what success looks like for this lab session.
